source_metadata.title_raw: Lynn Tracy Nerland - A-19-066 - July 26, 2019 - San Pablo

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Lynn Tracy Nerland 
City Attorney 
13831 San Pablo Avenue, Building 2 
San Pablo, CA 94806 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-066 
Dear Ms. Nerland: 
July 26, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding conflict of interest provisions of 
Government Code section 1090 ("Section 1090"). Please note that we are only providing advice 
under the conflict of interest provisions of Section 1090, not under other general conflict of interest 
p(ohibitions such as common law conflict of interest, including Public Contract Code. 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Contra Costa County 
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We did not receive a 
written response from either entity. (Section 1097.l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, 
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding 
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097 .1 ( c )( 5).) 
QUESTION 
Does Section 1090 prohibit a member of the San Pablo City Council from obtaining a loan 
through the City's First-Time Homebuyer Program ("Program") where the Council Member 
participated in the establishment of the Program? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. Section 1090 prohibits a member of the San Pablo City Council from obtaining a loan 
through the Program where the Council Member participated in the establishment of the Program. 


FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
File No. A-19-066 
Page No. 2 
Before the State dissolved Redevelopment Agencies, the San Pablo Redevelopment Agency 
had a first-time homebuyer program to provide down-payment assistance to qualified low and 
moderate income residents. These loans had low or no interest and typically were behind in priority 
to a commercial loan/mortgage. The program was instrumental in stabilizing housing insecurities 
and strengthening community bonds as homeowners tend to stay in the community. Accordingly, 
City staff has been exploring re-establishing a First-Time Homebuyer Program. 
In exploring options to re-establish a First-Time Homebuyers Program ("Program"), there 
have been discussions about contracting out administration of the Program to the nonprofit San 
Pablo Economic Development Corporation ("EDC"). The EDC has assisted the Federal Home Loan 
Bank of San Francisco as a community partner to member bank, Mechanics Bank, with the 
Workforce Initiative Subsidy for Homeownership Program, which is their first-time homebuyer's 
assistance program. Thus, the EDC staff has the experience that City staff does not have, given that 
the City no longer has redevelopment/housing staff since the State dissolution of redevelopment 
agencies. 
The EDC has an independent Board of Directors composed of five Directors. The City 
Council may nominate up to two people to sit on the Board as vacancies arise; however, they are 
subject to the standard nomination and election protocol, and the EDC Board may accept or reject 
these nominations. The City's Mayor currently serves on the Board. The EDC receives City funding 
for specific scopes of work in economic development, which constitutes 65% of its .income. It has 
its own Articles of Incorporation, bylaws and hires its own employees. The City Manager had 
previously served as a non-compensated Executive Director with the General Manager providing 
strategic and operational oversight. However, since July 1, 2018, the General Manager became the 
Executive Director, and the City Manager has no role. 
Under a contract, the City provides technical/IT support to the EDC, for which the EDC 
compensates the City. City staff and EDC staff work together on a number of projects for the 
common good of the community, notably small business development and workforce training. The 
EDC follows the Brown Act and its Board and designated staff members file Form 700s, per the 
City's Conflict of Interest Code. The Mayor who currently sits on the EDC Board does not 
participate in City decisions related to the EDC and the City Manager has recused himself as well. 
The concept for the Program is that the City Council would approve new program 
guidelines and general qualifications for participating in the Program ( e.g., loan underwriting 
requirements) and approve the contract with the EDC. The EDC would be responsible for program 
administration, including working directly with the applicant and appropriate parties (i.e., real estate 
broker, lender, title company). A Credit Committee would be formed by the two non-City-affiliated 
EDC Finance Committee members and one City staff member. It is envisioned that EDC staff will 
present the loan package and make a recommendation to the Credit Committee who will then make 
the decision whether to fund a loan. The City would ultimately sign the loan documents and fund 
the loan, and the EDC would receive fees through a successful loan funding. 
Like any loan underwriting program, there will be written loan guidelines based on income, 
assets, credit score, debt"to income ratio, as well as requirements for participation in a 


File No. A-19-066 
Page No. 3 
homebuyer/financial education program and preference for individuals who live or work in San 
Pablo. Unlike more conventional home mortgages, staff may look at extenuating circumstances of 
individual applicants. You anticipate some discretion in the recommendation and decision-making 
process perhaps beyond what a conventional home mortgage program through a bank may consider. 
For example, a potential borrower who meets the income and asset requirements may have a 
low credit score due to a prior foreclosure caused by a medical emergency. In such case, the 
applicant might still be considered if the household now appears to be able to make mortgage 
payments. San Pablo has a large percentage of low-income residents. With housing costs so high in 
the Bay Area, the goal of the program is to make home ownership more affordable for lower-
income households that are making rent payments and thus could make mortgage payments, but just 
do not have sufficient down payment funds available. 
The draft guidelines cap the loan of up to 10% of the home purchase price with a maximum 
of $50,000. The City will set aside monies when the loan program guidelines are adopted. The City 
may elect to set aside more monies ( or reduce funding) in the future. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 1090 codifies the common law prohibition against "self-dealing" with respect to 
governmental contracts. Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their 
official capacities, from making contracts in which they are financially interested. The prohibition 
applies regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Thomson 
v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) 
Section 1090 is concerned with financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, 
that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering 
the best interests of their agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Any renewal of a 
lease constitutes the "making" of a contract prohibited under Section 1090. (City of Imperial Seach 
v. Bailey (1996) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 
As a general rule, when Section 1090 applies to one member of a governing body of a 
public entity, as here, the prohibition cannot be avoided by having the interested official abstain; the 
entire governing body is precluded from entering into the contract. (Thomson, supra, at pp. 647-
649.) However, the Legislature has ameliorated the harsh consequences of Section 1090's 
prohibition by creating various statutory exceptions to Section 1090's prohibition where the interest 
involved is deemed a "remote interest," as defined in Section 1091 or a "nonint~rest," as defined in 
Section 1091. 5. If a remote interest is present, the contract may be made if, among other specified 
conditions, the officer abstains from any participation in the making of the contract. (Section 
1091(a).) If a noninterest is present, the officer may participate in the making of the contract, and 
the noninterest generally does not require disclosure. (City of Vernon v. Central Basin Mun. Water 
Dist. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 508, 514-515.) 
. The only exception that merits analysis given the facts presented is the noninterest exception 
set forth in Section 1091.5(a)(3) for "public services generally provided." That exception provides 
that an officer or employee "shall not be deemed to be interested" in a public contract if his or her 
interest in that contract is "[t]hat of a recipient of public services generally provided by the public 


File No. A-19-066 
Page No. 4 
body or board of which he or she is a member, on the same terms and conditions as ifhe or she 
were not a member of the body or board." 
Important to the present situation, the California Supreme Court considered the application 
of Section 1091.5(a)(3) in Lexin v. Superior Court (2010) 47 Cal.4th 1050, and opined on the 
phrase "on the same terms and conditions as ifhe or she were not a member of the body or board," 
which it characterized as a phrase codifying a "critical nondiscrimination principle." (Id. at p. 
1088.) Looking to other matters where the exception had been considered, it stated the exception 
had been applied "only because in each instance one could determine the officials were being 
treated the same as any other member of their agencies' constituencies." (Ibid; see, e.g., 88 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 122, 128 (2005), ["Anyone may pay for advertising space in the brochure at a 
predetermined rate ... " and the council member "would not receive a special rate or discount"]; 89 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 121, 124 (2006), ["Airport commissioners receive no priority to the hangar 
space and receive no preferential rental rate"].) 
The Supreme Court then stated that problems arise "when it is not possible to establish that 
an official's contract was untainted by favoritism arising from the· official's insider status." (Id. at p. 
1088.) To illustrate this point, it discussed an Attorney General opinion, 81 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 317 
(1998), concerning a city's small business loan program: 
. 
A city council member helped establish a municipal small 
business loan program and, after leaving office, sought to avail himself 
of the program by obtaining a loan. Problems of incommensurability 
again barred the transaction. Unlike contracts involving standard goods 
or services at set rates, "[ o ]btaining a government loan involves more 
complex considerations. The loan applicant must qualify, and the public 
official approving the loan must exercise some degree of discretion and 
judgment." (Id. at p. 320.) In such circumstances, it is impossible to 
demonstrate conclusively that other nonofficials would have received 
similar terms. Accordingly, section 1091.5(a)(3) would not shield the 
proposed loan from conflict of interest prohibitions.• 
These opinions establish, correctly, the principle that for any 
transaction to pass muster under section 1091.5(a)(3) it must be provable 
that no preferential treatment was involved ... There can be no special 
tailoring of a contract's terms, no discretion in determining what 
consideration a particular official must relinquish or may receive. 
(Id. at p. 1089.) 
Here, similar to the Attorney General opinion discussed in Lexin, the government contract at 
issue does not involve standard goods or services at set rates. Instead, it deals with the acquisition of 
a government loan which involves more complex considerations in that the applicant must qualify, 
and the individual approving the loan must exercise a certain degree of discretion and judgment. 
(See 81 Ops'.Cal.Atty.Gen. 317, 319.) This conclusion is reinforced by your facts which state that 
staff may look at extenuating circumstances of individual applicants when making 
recommendations to the Credit Committee who will determine whether to fund the loan. Such 


FileNo. A-19-066 
Page No. 5 
discretion would allow favoritism toward an official and occur on terms tailored to the official's 
loan application. Thus, the non-interest exception for public services does not apply to obtaining a 
government loan such as one under the Program here. (See 81 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 317, 320 (1998); 
see also Carillo Advice Letter, No. A-15-075.) 
You suggest the conclusion might be different given the City will contract with the EDC, a 
nonprofit, to administer the program, including exercising the discretion as to whether a particular 
loan should be funded as opposed to the City's staff. We note, however, the EDC receives 65% of 
its income from City funding and the City's Mayor currently sits on the EDC Board while the 
City's General Manager is the Executive Director. Moreover, EDC staff routinely works with City 
staff on several projects, and designated staff members of the EDC, and its Board, file Form 700s 
pursuant to the City's Conflict oflnterest Code. Finally, the EDC' Credit Committee, one member 
of which will be a City employee, will make the determination whether to fund a loan, but the City 
will ultimately sign the loan documents and fund the loan. 
, 
Under these circumstances, we do not view EDC's exercise of discretion in the loan process, 
as opposed to the City's, as a sufficient means to ensure no favoritism will occur with respect to 
loan applications from members of the City Council. In fact, with the City's significant 
participation in and influence over the EDC, the proposed arrangement is similar to situations where 
a body such as a city council has delegated its contracting authority to another individual or body 
but does not avoid application of Section 1090. (See, e.g;, 87 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 9 (2004) and 88 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 122 (2005).) 
Accordingly, members of the City Council would be prohibited by Section 1090 from 
obtaining First-Time Buyer loans under the proposed Program. 
Furthermore, this prohibition applies to the Council Members even after they no longer 
serve on the City Council. In a matter almost directly on point, the Attorney General's Office found 
that a city council member could not participate in the establishment of a loan program and then 
leave office and apply for a loan. (81 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 317, supra.) There, a former council 
member who had participated in the planning, discussions, and ,city approval necessary to establish 
a city loan program for developing businesses wanted to obtain a loan under the program he had 
helped to implement. 
Citing to Stigall, supra, the opinion noted that "a contract may be 'made' by a public officer 
or employee within the meaning of section 1090 even though the actual execution of the contract 
occurs after he or she leaves such public office or employment." (81 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 317, supra.) 
The opinion then explained that it is not necessary for an official to subjectively intend to contract 
with the agency after leaving office at the time he or she participates in setting up a government 
program (see 66 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen., supra, at 159-160); "[i]nstead, we have looked to whether the 
official had the opportunity and did participate in the policy decision to create the gove~ent 
program under which the contract would later be executed." 


File No. A-19-066 
Page No. 6 
Accordingly, members of the City Council are prohibited by Section 1090 from 
participating in the establishment the Program and then applying for a loan under the Program after · 
they are no longer serving on the City Council. 1 
JW:aja 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
6 ~ /Ji~ 
f::or 
By: 
Jack Woodside 
Counsel, Legal Division 
1 Because members of the City Council are conclusively presumed to have made all contracts under their 
jurisdiction for purposes of Section 1090, members of the City Council who abstained from participation in establishing 
the Program would nonetheless be prohibited from applying for loans under the Program after they leave office.

sections.question:
Does Section 1090 prohibit a member of the San Pablo City Council from obtaining a loan 
through the City's First-Time Homebuyer Program ("Program") where the Council Member 
participated in the establishment of the Program?
sections.conclusion:
Yes. Section 1090 prohibits a member of the San Pablo City Council from obtaining a loan 
through the Program where the Council Member participated in the establishment of the Program.

sections.analysis:
Section 1090 codifies the common law prohibition against "self-dealing" with respect to 
governmental contracts. Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their 
official capacities, from making contracts in which they are financially interested. The prohibition 
applies regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Thomson 
v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.)

that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering 
the best interests of their agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Any renewal of a 
lease constitutes the "making" of a contract prohibited under Section 1090. (City of Imperial Seach 
v. Bailey (1996) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 
As a general rule, when Section 1090 applies to one member of a governing body of a 
public entity, as here, the prohibition cannot be avoided by having the interested official abstain; the 
entire governing body is precluded from entering into the contract. (Thomson, supra, at pp. 647-
649.) However, the Legislature has ameliorated the harsh consequences of Section 1090's 
prohibition by creating various statutory exceptions to Section 1090's prohibition where the interest 
involved is deemed a "remote interest," as defined in Section 1091 or a "nonint~rest," as defined in

conditions, the officer abstains from any participation in the making of the contract. (Section 
1091(a).) If a noninterest is present, the officer may participate in the making of the contract, and

Dist. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 508, 514-515.) 
. The only exception that merits analysis given the facts presented is the noninterest exception 
set forth in Section 1091.5(a)(3) for "public services generally provided." That exception provides 
that an officer or employee "shall not be deemed to be interested" in a public contract if his or her

body or board of which he or she is a member, on the same terms and conditions as ifhe or she 
were not a member of the body or board."

of Section 1091.5(a)(3) in Lexin v. Superior Court (2010) 47 Cal.4th 1050, and opined on the

business loan program and, after leaving office, sought to avail himself

again barred the transaction. Unlike contracts involving standard goods 
or services at set rates, "[ o ]btaining a government loan involves more 
complex considerations. The loan applicant must qualify, and the public 
official approving the loan must exercise some degree of discretion and 
judgment." (Id. at p. 320.) In such circumstances, it is impossible to 
demonstrate conclusively that other nonofficials would have received 
similar terms. Accordingly, section 1091.5(a)(3) would not shield the 
proposed loan from conflict of interest prohibitions.• 
These opinions establish, correctly, the principle that for any 
transaction to pass muster under section 1091.5(a)(3) it must be provable 
that no preferential treatment was involved ... There can be no special 
tailoring of a contract's terms, no discretion in determining what

discretion would allow favoritism toward an official and occur on terms tailored to the official's

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does Section 1090 prohibit a member of the San Pablo City Council from obtaining a loan 
through the City's First-Time Homebuyer Program ("Program") where the Council Member 
participated in the establishment of the Program?

CONCLUSION: Yes. Section 1090 prohibits a member of the San Pablo City Council from obtaining a loan 
through the Program where the Council Member participated in the establishment of the Program.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION • 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 Lynn Tracy Nerland City Attorney 13831 San Pablo Avenue, Building 2 San Pablo, CA 94806 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-19-066 Dear Ms. Nerland: July 26, 2019 This letter responds to your request for advice regarding conflict of interest provisions of Government Code section 1090 ("Section 1090"). Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of Section 1090, not under other general conflict of interest p(ohibitions such as common law conflict of interest, including Public Contract Code. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Contra Costa County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097 .1 ( c )( 5).) QUESTION Does Section 1090 prohibit a member of the San Pablo City Council from obtaining a loan through the City's First-Time Homebuyer Program ("Program") where the Council Member participated in the establishment of the Program? CONCLUSION Yes. Section 1090 prohibits a member of the San Pablo City Council from obtaining a loan through the Program where the Council Member participated in the establishment of the Program. FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER File No. A-19-066 Page No. 2 Before the State dissolved Redevelopment Agencies, the San Pablo Redevelopment Agency had a first-time homebuyer program to provide down-payment assistance to qualified low and moderate income residents. These loans had low or no interest and typically were behind in priority to a commercial loan/mortgage. The program was instrumental in stabilizing housing insecurities and strengthening community bonds as homeowners tend to stay in the community. Accordingly, City staff has been exploring re-establishing a First-Time Homebuyer Program. In exploring options to re-establish a First-Time Homebuyers Program ("Program"), there have been discussions about contracting out administration of the Program to the nonprofit San Pablo Economic Development Corporation ("EDC"). The EDC has assisted the Federal Home Loan Bank of San Francisco as a community partner to member bank, Mechanics Bank, with the Workforce Initiative Subsidy for Homeownership Program, which is their first-time homebuyer's assistance program.
